metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'Verify by reading' instruction in AC3 with concrete grep commands that assert `sign_review` appears under the Write-gated section header (not the LLM-gated header), `reconcile` has a JIRA-SYNC annotation, and `fsck` has a READONLY annotation — e.g. `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && awk '/Write-gated/,/LLM-gated/' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review' || echo 'FAIL: sign_review not in write-gated'`.
findings.finding: AC3's verification for special-case placement is 'Verify by reading the generated sections' — a manual human-inspection step, not a concrete deterministic proving command. The three special cases (`sign_review` in write-gated not llm-gated, `reconcile` annotated jira-gated, `fsck` readonly-gated) are the highest-risk classification decisions in the plan, yet they have no shell-executable check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 3-vs-4 section contradiction: either add a fourth label to the AC grep, or explicitly state that `reconcile` is annotated within the Read-only section (not a separate section heading), so the generator's structural output is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section describes FOUR output sections ('Read-only (always)', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated', and 'the jira-gated reconcile live mode'), but AC line 2's shell verification only tests for THREE section labels ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). It is unspecified whether the jira-gated reconcile content is a standalone fourth section heading, a subsection of Read-only, or an inline annotation — leaving the generator's structural output protocol ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope (four-section description) vs. AC line 2 (three-section grep check)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `'jira-gated'` or `'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'` as a fourth term in the AC line 2 grep, OR (b) reword the Scope to say 'three sections, with reconcile annotated in-line within the Read-only section' — pick one and make the AC match.

findings.finding: The Scope names FOUR output sections ('Read-only (always)', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated', and a fourth jira-gated reconcile section), but AC2's shell verify only checks for THREE section labels ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). An implementer could omit the fourth section and pass all ACs while violating the Scope.
findings.location: Scope (four sections) vs AC2 (three-section grep)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's loop: `for s in 'Read-only' 'LLM-gated' 'Write-gated' 'Jira'; do grep -qi "$s" docs/mcp-reference.md || echo "NO SECTION $s"; done`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's count-proof command to escape or single-quote the backtick characters in the grep pattern so shell expansion cannot corrupt it (e.g. use single quotes: `grep -cE $'^\\| ?`?[a-z_]+`? '` or replace the backtick-optional anchors with `[\`]?`).
findings.finding: The AC4 count-proof command embeds literal backtick characters inside a bash double-quoted `$(...)` expansion. In bash, backticks inside `$(...)` are still interpreted as command substitution, so the grep pattern `'^| ?`?[a-z_]+`? '` will trigger a nested command substitution on the fragment `?[a-z_]+` rather than being passed literally to grep, causing the command to error or silently produce a wrong count — the proving command silently lies.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 count proof command
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the double-quoted grep pattern with a single-quoted one to prevent backtick interpolation:
```bash
python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && [ "$(grep -cE '^[|] ?[`]?[a-z_]+[`]? ' docs/mcp-reference.md)" -ge 47 ] || echo "TOO FEW TOOLS"
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an automated assertion in the AC (or as a unit test) that verifies correct section placement of the three special-case tools: e.g., `grep -A50 'Write-gated' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review'` and `grep -A10 'reconcile' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'` — so misclassification is caught by CI, not only by human review.
findings.finding: AC3's verification of correct special-case placement (`sign_review` in write-gated tier, `reconcile` annotated as jira-gated, `fsck` readonly-gated) is specified as '(Verify by reading the generated sections)' — a manual step — with no automated assertion. The classification override table is the most non-trivial logic in the generator; a regression that misclassifies these tools would pass all CI checks undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py classification logic
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend AC3 with runnable shell assertions, for example:
```
grep -A 60 'Write-gated' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -qE 'sign_review' || echo 'MISCLASSIFIED sign_review'
grep -E 'reconcile' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' || echo 'MISSING jira annotation on reconcile'
grep -E 'fsck' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY' || echo 'MISSING readonly annotation on fsck'
```
These can live in the AC shell block or as parametrized assertions in `tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py`.

findings.finding: AC3's verification of the most logic-rich part of the generator — the three-entry special-case override table (sign_review in write-gated tier, reconcile annotated jira-gated, fsck readonly-gated) — is left as a manual 'Verify by reading the generated sections' step with no automated assertion in make test or pytest. If the classification logic is wrong, CI stays green.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'The three special cases are correctly placed/annotated … (Verify by reading the generated sections.)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`, run `gen_mcp_reference.py` (or call its public `generate()` function directly), read the output string, and use `assert` to verify section membership and annotation text for each of the three special-case tools. Parametrize for clarity.

findings.finding: The special-case placement AC relies solely on a manual human inspection oracle ('Verify by reading the generated sections') for the most error-prone classification rule (`sign_review` in write-gated tier despite being in the LLM registrar). No deterministic shell command or automated assertion verifies this placement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 3: 'Verify by reading the generated sections.'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add three grep-based AC commands:
```sh
# sign_review must appear under the Write-gated section, not LLM-gated
python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py
awk '/Write-gated/,/^##/' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review' || echo 'FAIL: sign_review not in write-gated section'
# reconcile must be annotated with REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC
grep -q 'reconcile.*REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC\|REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC.*reconcile' docs/mcp-reference.md || echo 'FAIL: reconcile jira annotation missing'
# fsck must appear in the read-only section with readonly annotation
awk '/Read-only/,/^##/' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'fsck' || echo 'FAIL: fsck not in read-only section'
```

findings.finding: The output section count is inconsistently specified: the Scope says FOUR labeled sections (Read-only, LLM-gated, Write-gated, and a separate jira-gated reconcile section), but the AC verification command checks only THREE section names ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). The plan never resolves whether the jira-gated reconcile live mode is a standalone fourth section or an inline annotation within the Read-only section — leaving the generator's output structure decision rule ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope ('FOUR clearly-labeled sections') vs. AC bullet 2 (grep checks only 3: 'Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope, change 'FOUR clearly-labeled sections' to 'THREE clearly-labeled sections, with reconcile annotated within the Read-only section as jira-gated (REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC) for its live mode.' This aligns with AC bullet 3's 'annotated' language and keeps reconcile structurally in the Read-only section (where it is registered) while calling out its live-mode gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the AC4 count assertion to exclude header rows — either use a stricter pattern that excludes section-header table rows, use `== 47` instead of `>= 47` with a pattern verified to exclude headers, or add a direct Python assertion in the generator's `--check` mode that prints the exact count and asserts it equals 47.
findings.finding: The AC4 count threshold `>= 47` is susceptible to silent under-counting because markdown table header rows (one per section, 4 sections) match the same grep pattern `^| ?[backtick]?[a-z_]+[backtick]? ` as tool rows, inflating the real tool count by up to 4. If the generator emits only 43 tool rows but 4 header rows (e.g., `| tool | description | gate |`), the check still passes at 47, hiding a missing-write-tools bug.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 count proof command
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the generator's `--check` output a line like `Tools: 47` and assert on that structured output: `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check 2>&1 | grep -qE 'Tools: 47' || echo 'COUNT WRONG'`.

findings.finding: The plan declares FOUR output sections but AC2's shell verification only checks for THREE section labels, and the fourth section's structural form is ambiguous — the plan alternately describes it as a separate section ('jira-gated reconcile live mode') and as an annotation within the Read-only section. No output schema for how multi-gate tools (reconcile: always listed, but REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC for live modes) are rendered in the table is specified.
findings.location: Scope section (four sections) / AC2 shell verification

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section exactly how the generator constructs the ctx SimpleNamespace for each registrar (e.g., 'import _readonly/_allow_llm/_allow_jira_sync/_dump/logger from mcp_server, import MODE_CAPS/Mode from mcp_server, set REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 in os.environ before importing, and build ctx = SimpleNamespace(readonly=_readonly, allow_llm=_allow_llm, …)'), to prevent silent write-tool under-counting from a wrongly constructed ctx.
findings.finding: The generator must construct a ctx SimpleNamespace to pass to each registrar independently, but the plan does not specify how ctx is built outside build_server(). The reads registrar needs ctx.MODE_CAPS and ctx.Mode (for reconcile), which requires _load_engine_mode() to have run; the writes registrar needs ctx.readonly() to return False (to allow registration); all registrars need ctx.allow_llm, ctx.allow_jira_sync, ctx.dump, ctx.logger. The plan's phrase 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP' implies direct calling, but omits the ctx construction recipe — a developer must either (a) import and call _load_engine_mode() explicitly, (b) partially import from mcp_server.py (triggering the module-level execution), or (c) mock the ctx fields. A wrong ctx (e.g. ctx.readonly()=True) would cause register_write_tools to silently no-op, producing an under-counted 29-tool output without error, violating AC1 and AC4.
findings.location: Scope section: 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 (writes register)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The generator imports mcp_server (triggering _load_engine_mode()) to obtain MODE_CAPS, Mode, and the gate helpers, then sets os.environ["REBAR_MCP_READONLY"] = "0" before calling register_write_tools to ensure ctx.readonly() returns False. It calls each registrar on a fresh FastMCP() instance and collects tool names via list_tools().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether sign_review is MOVED to the Write-gated section (LLM section then shows 5 tools) or appears in both sections with cross-references, and add a per-section count assertion to the ACs (or explicitly state the expected per-section totals after overrides are applied).
findings.finding: The override table says `sign_review -> REBAR_MCP_READONLY` tier, but sign_review is in the LLM registrar. The plan states it 'appears in the write-gated (REBAR_MCP_READONLY) tier NOT the llm-gated tier', which implies it is MOVED out of the LLM section entirely. However, the plan never specifies what happens to the LLM registrar's tool count when sign_review is moved: the 'LLM-gated' section would then show 5 tools (not 6), and the 'Write-gated' section 19 (not 18). No count reconciliation rule is given for overridden tools, so the AC's count verification (`>= 47 rows`) would still pass but the per-section counts in the doc would silently mismatch the plan's stated registrar totals.
findings.location: Classification rule table (LLM group / sign_review override) and AC line 1 (count proof)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a guard comment (or inline assertion) in `gen_mcp_reference.py` that documents exactly when the override table must be updated, and consider adding a count assertion (e.g., `assert len(overrides) == 3`) so adding a new hybrid tool without updating the table fails loudly rather than silently mis-classifying.
findings.finding: The 3-entry special-case override table (reconcile→JIRA-gate, fsck→readonly-gate, sign_review→write-gate) is described as 'closed' but will be hardcoded in gen_mcp_reference.py with no per-tool attribute in the registrars. If a future tool is added to the LLM registrar with write-gate semantics (a hybrid like sign_review), the generator silently mis-classifies it — and the drift gate only catches count/name drift, NOT classification-logic errors.
findings.location: Scope section — 'classify via the table above'; Classification rule table
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of the override dict in gen_mcp_reference.py, add a block comment: '# CLOSED override table — update when a tool's effective gate differs from its registrar default. # Currently 3 entries: reconcile (jira-gate), fsck (readonly-gate), sign_review (write-gate in LLM registrar). # If you add a hybrid-gated tool without updating this table, classification will be wrong.' Optionally add `assert len(GATE_OVERRIDES) == 3, 'Update this assertion when adding a new hybrid-gated tool'` so CI fails loudly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` wraps registrar loading in explicit error handling that emits a clear message (e.g., 'ERROR: failed to load register_write_tools: <reason>') and exits non-zero, and that missing/None `.description` falls back to a placeholder rather than crashing.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for registrar import failures or exceptions thrown during FastMCP instantiation/tool registration. If any registrar module fails to import (e.g., missing dependency, syntax error introduced in a refactor), the generator crashes with a raw Python traceback rather than a clear, actionable error message, and CI fails with a confusing signal.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the Files list and update it with an entry for `docs/mcp-reference.md` (mirroring the `env-vars.md` entry in the Operator or Agent section).
findings.finding: docs/README.md is an unenumerated consumer that will be left stale. It maintains a curated, audience-grouped index of every file in docs/ — including an explicit entry for docs/env-vars.md (the nearest parallel). Adding docs/mcp-reference.md without a corresponding entry in docs/README.md leaves the Agent or Operator audience with no discovery path to the new reference.
findings.location: Scope / Files section — lists docs/mcp-reference.md as new but omits docs/README.md
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/README.md` to the plan's Files list and, in the Agent (or Operator) section of that file, add: `- **[mcp-reference.md](mcp-reference.md)** — the generated reference of all MCP tools, classified by gate tier (always / LLM-gated / write-gated); kept in sync by a CI drift gate.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'mcp-reference drift gate' entry to _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES in tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py (e.g., 'mcp-reference drift gate': 'gen_mcp_reference.py') so the named-step parity test enforces this gate's presence in both CI files long-term, not just the regex check.
findings.finding: AC6 states 'python -m pytest tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py -q passes' as sufficient parity proof, but only the test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci test (regex-based) will auto-enforce scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py. The named-gate parity test (test_verified_gate_runs_every_shared_gate) checks _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES entries — a hardcoded dict that does not include 'gen_mcp_reference'. Adding gen_mcp_reference to both CI files will make the regex test pass, but the named-step test will still pass vacuously (it only checks existing entries). If a future refactor renames/removes the step in one file, the named-step test won't catch it because there's no entry for it. The plan should note that _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES also needs a new entry.
findings.location: AC6: 'python -m pytest tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py -q passes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified fallback for absent/None tool `.description` (e.g., emit `'(no description)'` or raise an explicit error that fails the generator with a clear message).
findings.finding: The plan specifies reading each tool's 'first-line summary (from `.description`)' but provides no fallback for tools whose `.description` is `None`, empty, or whose docstring's first line is blank. FastMCP tools can have no docstring. No fallback behavior (skip, placeholder string, error) is defined, so generator behavior on description-absent tools is unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section: 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope: 'If a tool's `.description` is None or empty, emit `(no description)` as the summary cell and log a warning to stderr.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` writes to a tempfile and atomically renames it to `docs/mcp-reference.md` so a crash mid-write never leaves a partial file.
findings.finding: The generator writes `docs/mcp-reference.md` in-place with no atomic write (write-to-tempfile + rename). A crash or KeyboardInterrupt mid-write leaves a partially written file committed or present on disk, which may pass or silently corrupt the drift gate comparison.
findings.location: Scope section — `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (new); AC drift-gate verification
findings.suggested_fix: In the script spec, add: write full content to a `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile` in the same directory, then `os.replace(tmp.name, 'docs/mcp-reference.md')` — this is atomic on POSIX and prevents partial-file states.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `mcp` must be added to the `[dev]` extra (so existing CI `.[dev]` installs gain it automatically) or whether each drift-gate CI step must install `.[mcp]` separately — and update the plan/CI accordingly so the generator can import FastMCP in the gate environment.
findings.finding: The generator creates FastMCP instances by importing from `mcp.server.fastmcp`, which requires the optional `[mcp]` extra. The plan's CI gate runs `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` in the same step that installs `.[dev]`, but the plan does not specify that `mcp` must be added to the `[dev]` extra or that the CI step must install `.[mcp]`. If the `mcp` extra is absent from the CI install, the generator will raise ImportError and the drift gate will fail with a misleading error on every run — not because the reference is stale, but because the dependency is missing.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP'; AC line 6 ('The gate is wired in BOTH CI files')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `mcp` to the `[dev]` optional-dependencies group in `pyproject.toml` (the lowest-blast-radius option, since the mcp package is already a declared optional extra and dev machines often have it), or add `pip install -e '.[dev,mcp]'` to the drift-gate step. Note in the plan which approach is chosen.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` requires (and should assert) the `mcp` extra is installed, or document the expected fallback behavior (clear ImportError message, non-zero exit) so CI wiring is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a fallback for the case where the generator's per-registrar FastMCP enumeration fails at import time — e.g., when `mcp` (FastMCP) is not installed in the CI environment running `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py`. The referenced pattern `gen_env_registry.py` uses only stdlib (ast/argparse) and has no such dependency, but this generator requires instantiating FastMCP. A missing `mcp` extra in CI silently produces a zero-tool output that passes `--check` against a previously-generated file.
findings.location: Scope section (generator approach) and AC line 4 (count proof) and AC line 6 (CI wiring)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope: 'The generator must fail fast with a clear error if `mcp` is not installed (mirror the SystemExit in build_server), and the CI job that wires it must install rebar[mcp].'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
